subject: infectious-diseases
title: Sepsis
preambleMarkdown:
# Sepsis



> **Clinical-review notice:** This is a bullet-first examination and clinical-orientation draft. Local protocols, medication doses, procedural thresholds, pregnancy/pediatric pathways, emergency workflows, and jurisdiction-specific guidance must be checked before clinical use.

sections.heading: Overview
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Sepsis is an infectious syndrome or organism-related topic in infectious diseases.
- This article focuses on organism, exposure, syndrome, diagnostics, antimicrobial selection, isolation, and prevention.
- The practical clinical priority is host-pathogen interaction, source control, and timely antimicrobial decisions.
- Start by deciding whether the presentation is stable, time-sensitive, complicated, recurrent, or incidental.
- Imported source notes are preserved below for clinical review and citation reconciliation.

sections.heading: Key Points
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Identify unstable, time-sensitive, or function-threatening presentations first.
- Anchor the assessment of Sepsis to the patient context: age, pregnancy status when relevant, immune status, comorbid disease, medications, prior episodes, and local resources.
- Use focused history and examination before broad testing.
- Choose investigations that change diagnosis, severity assessment, treatment, disposition, or follow-up.
- Treat reversible danger early and give clear safety-net instructions.

sections.heading: Definition
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Sepsis is best defined by the specific clinical problem, anatomic site, mechanism, severity, and care setting.
- For this topic, the core concept is host-pathogen interaction, source control, and timely antimicrobial decisions.
- Separate suspected, confirmed, recurrent, complicated, and resolved disease states.

sections.heading: Classification
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Community-acquired, healthcare-associated, travel-related, device-related, or opportunistic infection.
- Localized, disseminated, recurrent, latent, or postinfectious presentation.
- Bacterial, viral, fungal, parasitic, toxin-mediated, or mixed infection.
- Uncomplicated disease, severe disease, sepsis, source-control need, or outbreak/public-health concern.

sections.heading: Epidemiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- The epidemiology of Sepsis depends on age, sex, geography, exposure, comorbid disease, medication use, pregnancy status when relevant, and ascertainment setting.
- In infectious diseases, exam questions usually test the common population plus the high-risk exception.
- Risk estimates should be updated from local data or current guideline sources before publication.

sections.heading: Anatomy
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Identify the organ, tissue, vessel, nerve, compartment, joint, pathway, or system most directly involved in Sepsis.
- Map nearby structures that explain referred pain, spread, obstruction, ischemia, neurologic deficits, imaging findings, or procedure risk.
- For non-anatomic topics, focus on the structure most likely to change symptoms, testing, or treatment.

sections.heading: Physiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Define the normal function that Sepsis disrupts or depends on.
- Connect the physiology to symptoms, examination findings, laboratory abnormalities, imaging appearance, and treatment targets.
- Consider compensation, reserve, feedback loops, and decompensation triggers.

sections.heading: Pathophysiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- The central mechanism in Sepsis should explain why the patient develops the key symptoms and complications.
- Mechanisms may include obstruction, inflammation, ischemia, infection, immune injury, degeneration, trauma, thrombosis, endocrine imbalance, toxicity, neoplasia, or functional dysregulation.
- Distinguish the primary process from secondary complications and treatment-related harm.

sections.heading: Etiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Consider the common causes of Sepsis first, then actively check for dangerous but less common causes.
- Review congenital, acquired, infectious, inflammatory, traumatic, metabolic, neoplastic, medication-related, toxic, pregnancy-related, immune, and idiopathic causes when relevant.
- Use the time course and exposure history to narrow the cause.

sections.heading: Risk Factors
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Risk changes with age, family history, baseline disease, immune status, pregnancy, medications, procedures, travel, occupation, environment, and prior episodes.
- For Sepsis, risk factors should guide pretest probability and prevention, not replace objective assessment.
- Document modifiable risks that can be addressed during treatment or follow-up.

sections.heading: Genetics
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Consider inherited disease when presentation is early, familial, recurrent, syndromic, unusually severe, or associated with congenital findings.
- Ask about family history, ancestry when clinically relevant, consanguinity, pregnancy losses, and similar symptoms in relatives.
- Use genetic testing when results change diagnosis, screening, treatment, reproductive counseling, or family risk assessment.

sections.heading: Microbiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Consider infection when fever, exposure, immunocompromise, procedure, device, wound, systemic inflammation, or rapid clinical decline is present.
- Identify the likely source, route of spread, organism group, need for cultures, need for isolation, and need for source control.
- Antimicrobial decisions should account for severity, host risk, allergies, renal or hepatic function, pregnancy status, local resistance, and prior cultures.

sections.heading: Clinical Presentation
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Fever, localizing symptoms, exposure history, immune status, device or procedure history, and severity define the syndrome.
- Immunocompromised patients, neonates, pregnancy, and older adults may lack classic symptoms.
- Sepsis, airway threat, neurologic involvement, necrosis, and rapidly progressive disease change urgency.

sections.heading: History
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Ask onset, duration, progression, triggers, exposures, associated symptoms, prior episodes, and treatments already tried.
- Review medications, allergies, procedures, devices, travel, occupation, sexual history when relevant, pregnancy status when relevant, family history, and baseline function.
- For Sepsis, ask targeted questions that separate uncomplicated disease from dangerous complications.

sections.heading: Physical Examination
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Start with vital signs, mental status, perfusion, respiratory effort, hydration, pain severity, and overall appearance.
- Perform a focused infectious diseases examination that directly tests the suspected mechanism.
- Look for systemic involvement, complications, mimics, and findings that change urgency or disposition.

sections.heading: Red Flags
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Shock, altered mental status, respiratory distress, severe hypoxemia, syncope, seizure, major bleeding, or rapidly progressive symptoms.
- Severe pain out of proportion, new neurologic deficit, threatened airway, sepsis, peritonitis, limb ischemia, compartment syndrome, or vision threat.
- Any Sepsis presentation with organ dysfunction, pregnancy risk, immunocompromise, failed initial treatment, or diagnostic uncertainty needs a lower threshold for escalation.

sections.heading: Differential Diagnosis
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Compare Sepsis with disorders that share the same dominant symptom, anatomic site, exposure, laboratory pattern, or imaging abnormality.
- Prioritize dangerous mimics that would be harmed by delay or by the wrong treatment.
- Use acuity, location, risk factors, examination findings, labs, imaging, and response to treatment to narrow the differential.

sections.heading: Diagnostic Approach
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- First decide whether Sepsis is unstable, complicated, recurrent, incidental, or safe for outpatient workup.
- Use the highest-yield initial test for the suspected syndrome rather than ordering broad panels without a decision point.
- If early tests are negative but suspicion remains high, reassess the history, repeat focused examination, and choose second-line testing based on the remaining differential.

sections.heading: Investigations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Use labs to assess severity, complications, treatment safety, and alternative diagnoses.
- Use imaging, bedside tests, cultures, tissue sampling, functional testing, or procedures when results change management.
- Interpret results with pretest probability and clinical context.
- Avoid tests that add risk, delay, or incidental findings without changing the next step.

sections.heading: Diagnostic Criteria
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Diagnosis of Sepsis should combine compatible clinical pattern with accepted laboratory, imaging, procedural, histologic, physiologic, or scoring criteria when available.
- Do not diagnose from one nonspecific abnormality when the full syndrome does not fit.
- Document uncertainty and the follow-up plan when the diagnosis remains provisional.

sections.heading: Severity Classification
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Severity depends on stability, organ dysfunction, symptom burden, anatomic extent, complications, treatment risk, and ability to follow up.
- Identify who needs urgent referral, admission, ICU care, procedure, surgery, isolation, rehabilitation, or close outpatient review.
- Use validated disease-specific scoring or staging systems when they exist and are available locally.

sections.heading: Management
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Stabilize sepsis, obtain high-yield cultures when they will not delay care, and start empiric therapy when indicated.
- Narrow therapy once organism, susceptibility, source, and response are known.
- Add source control, isolation, prophylaxis, vaccination, contact tracing, or public-health notification when relevant.

### Initial Management

- Confirm whether Sepsis is stable, unstable, incidental, recurrent, or complicated.
- Assess airway, breathing, circulation, mental status, pain, hydration, perfusion, and immediate threats.
- Escalate early when red flags, organ dysfunction, procedural need, or diagnostic uncertainty is present.

### Non-pharmacological Management

- Use education, observation, rehabilitation, isolation, procedural planning, or environmental change when it addresses host-pathogen interaction, source control, and timely antimicrobial decisions.
- Tailor activity, diet, wound, exposure, work, driving, return-to-play, or safety advice to the clinical setting.

### Pharmacological Management

- Start empiric antimicrobial therapy promptly when severe infection, sepsis, meningitis, necrotizing infection, or immunocompromised infection is suspected.
- Verify dose, route, duration, interactions, allergies, renal and hepatic adjustment, pregnancy or lactation status, and monitoring.

### Procedures

- Drain abscesses, remove infected devices, debride necrotic tissue, decompress obstruction, or obtain diagnostic samples when source control is needed.
- Check consent, anatomy, imaging, bleeding risk, infection risk, sedation risk, equipment, and aftercare before proceeding.

### Surgical Management

- Surgical or procedural referral is needed for instability, failed medical management, obstruction, perforation, ischemia, uncontrolled bleeding, malignancy concern, repairable structural disease, or unsafe bedside management.
- Timing depends on severity, anatomy, operative risk, patient goals, and local expertise.

sections.heading: Monitoring
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Track symptoms, vital signs, function, pain, intake/output when relevant, focused examination findings, key labs, adverse effects, and response to therapy.
- For Sepsis, monitoring should detect deterioration, treatment toxicity, recurrence, and missed complications.
- Escalate if expected improvement does not occur or new red flags appear.

sections.heading: Follow-up
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Confirm diagnosis, severity, treatment response, adherence, side effects, and patient understanding.
- Arrange repeat examination, labs, imaging, rehabilitation, surveillance, medication review, or specialty care when indicated.
- Give a clear reassessment interval and explicit return precautions.

sections.heading: Complications
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Complications of Sepsis may be acute, chronic, recurrent, treatment-related, functional, psychosocial, or procedure-related.
- Prioritize complications that threaten life, airway, limb, vision, fertility, neurologic function, organ function, or public health.
- Document warning symptoms that require urgent reassessment.

sections.heading: Prognosis
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Prognosis depends on cause, severity, timing of recognition, comorbid disease, age, treatment response, complications, and follow-up reliability.
- Early recognition of dangerous Sepsis patterns improves disposition and reduces preventable complications.
- Published prognostic statistics should be checked against current specialty sources before release.

sections.heading: Prevention
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Address modifiable risk factors, medication safety, vaccination or prophylaxis when relevant, exposure reduction, screening, and recurrence prevention.
- For Sepsis, prevention should target the cause, the complication pathway, and the follow-up failure points.
- Use patient-specific counseling rather than generic reassurance.

sections.heading: Patient Education
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Explain Sepsis in plain language, including what is known, what remains uncertain, and what symptoms should trigger urgent care.
- Review medication purpose, expected benefit, side effects, duration, and what to do if symptoms worsen.
- Confirm the patient understands follow-up, restrictions, wound or device care when relevant, and return precautions.

sections.heading: Nursing Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Monitor vital signs, pain, function, mental status, intake/output when relevant, wounds or devices, and response to treatment.
- Escalate fever, bleeding, respiratory change, neurologic change, perfusion change, uncontrolled pain, medication reaction, or worsening function.
- Reinforce education, discharge instructions, safe mobility, adherence, and follow-up logistics.

sections.heading: Pharmacy Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Check allergies, renal and hepatic function, pregnancy or lactation status, interactions, duplication, contraindications, adherence barriers, and monitoring needs.
- For Sepsis, medication review should include both treatment drugs and drugs that may cause, worsen, or mimic the problem.
- High-risk drugs require a documented indication, duration, monitoring plan, and counseling.

sections.heading: Dentistry Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Ask about bleeding risk, anticoagulants, antiplatelets, immunosuppression, prosthetic material, active infection, and relevant medication adverse effects before invasive dental work.
- Coordinate with the treating clinician when medication interruption, prophylaxis, or infection control is being considered.
- Sepsis does not automatically imply dental antibiotic prophylaxis; use condition-specific indications and local guidance.

sections.heading: Special Populations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Pediatrics: use age-specific normal values, developmental context, weight-based dosing, caregiver education, and safeguarding awareness.
- Pregnancy: stabilize the mother first, choose pregnancy-aware testing and medications when possible, and involve obstetrics for significant disease.
- Older adults: consider frailty, atypical presentation, falls, delirium, kidney function, polypharmacy, cognition, and goals of care.
- Renal or hepatic impairment: adjust diagnostic contrast, medications, dosing, and monitoring.
- Immunocompromised patients: lower the threshold for atypical infection, severe disease, cultures, imaging, admission, and interaction review.

sections.heading: Guideline Comparison
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Current guidance for Sepsis may vary by country, specialty society, age group, pregnancy status, disease severity, and available resources.
- Compare local policy with major society guidance for diagnostic thresholds, treatment sequencing, drug choice, procedural timing, and follow-up intervals.
- Document where local practice differs from international guidance and why.

sections.heading: Clinical Pearls
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Stabilize first when the patient is unstable; diagnostic precision comes after immediate threats are controlled.
- Match each test to a decision it will change.
- Reassess after treatment because nonresponse is often a diagnostic clue.
- For Sepsis, the highest-yield clinical move is to identify severity and the next safe action.

sections.heading: Common Mistakes
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Treating Sepsis as a label instead of confirming mechanism, severity, and complications.
- Ordering broad tests without a focused question.
- Missing red flags because the initial symptom sounds common.
- Ignoring pregnancy status, renal function, hepatic function, allergies, interactions, or follow-up barriers.
- Discharging without clear return precautions.

sections.heading: Exam Pearls
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Step 1: know the mechanism, anatomy, microbiology, pharmacology, or physiology behind the topic.
- Step 2 CK: know the next best diagnostic or management step.
- Step 3: know disposition, monitoring, complications, follow-up, and patient safety.
- Board exams: know contraindications, high-risk exceptions, and when to escalate.

sections.heading: High-Yield Facts
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Do not delay antimicrobials in unstable sepsis.
- Cultures are most valuable before antibiotics when feasible, but stabilization comes first.
- Source control can matter as much as antimicrobial choice.

sections.heading: Evidence Gaps
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- This Sepsis draft still needs specialty review before publication.
- Medication doses, procedural thresholds, screening intervals, risk scores, and guideline claims should be localized.
- Imported notes, when present, should be reconciled with current evidence and cited sources.

sections.heading: References
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Current specialty society guidance, local protocols, and institutional pathways should be checked during clinical review.
- When imported source notes are present, reconcile them against current references before publication.

### Source Excerpt: Sepsis

_Source lines: 5488-5549._

```text
Sepsis
- When have a suspected source of infection and SIRS is present
Septic Shock
- Hypotension induced by sepsis persisting despite adequate fluid
resuscitation
Multiple Organ Dysfunction Syndrome (MODS)
- Altered organ function in an acutely ill patient, usually leading to death

Neurogenic Shock
A. General Characteristics
1. Neurogenic shock results from a failure of the sympathetic nervous
system to maintain adequate vascular tone (sympathetic
denervation)
2. Causes include spinal cord injury, severe head injury, spinal
anesthesia, pharmacologic sympathetic blockade
3. Characterized by peripheral vasodilation with decreased SVR
B. Clinical Features
1. Warm, well-perfused skin
2. Urine output low or normal
3. Bradycardia and hypotension (but tachycardia can occur)
4. Cardiac output is decreased, SVR low, PCWP low to normal
C. Treatment
1. Judicious use of IV fluids as the mainstay of treatment
2. Vasoconstrictors to restore venous tone, but cautiously

## 3. Supine or Trendelenburg position

4. Maintain body temperature
Obstructive Shock
A. General Characteristics
1. Caused by an obstruction of circulation. Most commonly due to
massive PE or limited filling of the heart due to extrinsic pressure
from pericardium (cardiac tamponade) or thoracic cavity (tension
pneumothorax).

2. Cardiac output limited by obstruction, SVR makes a compensatory
increase to maintain systemic blood pressure.

3. Usually manifests with elevated JVP due to limited filling of right
ventricle or obstruction of pulmonary circulation by PE.

4. Treatment requires diagnosis of underlying disease. Cardiac
tamponade and tension pneumothorax are clinical diagnoses that
can be made at bedside.

5. Supportive treatment usually involves giving fluid and/or
vasopressors to maintain blood pressures until definitive therapy
can be given.

6. Cardiac tamponade: Requires pericardiocentesis or pericardial
window.

7. Tension pneumothorax: Requires needle thoracostomy followed by
chest tube placement.

8. Massive PE: Requires thrombolysis (systemic or catheter-directed
tPA) or thrombectomy.
```

<!-- source: lib.md import end -->

### Source Excerpt: Sepsis

_Source lines: 12437-12692._

```text
I. SEPSIS
A. Discussion. Sepsis should be considered a clinical syndrome. Sepsis is defined
as meeting two of systemic inflammatory response syndrome criteria plus a
source of infection (see Clinical Pearl 6-1). Over 1,665,000 cases of sepsis occur
in the United States every year, with a mortality rate of up to 50% (see Clinical
Pearl 6-2). Early goal-directed therapy has revolutionized sepsis management, and
sepsis bundles have shown promise in improving mortality rates.

B. Clinical features vary considerably depending on whether the patient is in the
early, intermediate, or late phase of the syndrome.

1. General symptoms and physical examination findings
a. Systemic findings may include fever, chills, rigors, or hypothermia.

b. Neuromuscular findings may include myalgias and arthralgias.

c. Neurologic findings may include altered mental status.

d. Cardiopulmonary findings may include tachycardia, arrhythmias,
hypotension, hypertension, a widened pulse pressure, or tachypnea.

e. Dermatologic findings. Dermal lesions that should increase clinical
suspicion of sepsis include petechiae, embolic lesions, and ecthyma
gangrenosum.

2. Site-specific symptoms and physical examination findings
a. Skin. Findings that might be indicative of the initial focal source include
erythema, a localized increase in temperature, lymphadenitis, induration,
fluctuation, and pus.

b. Heart or lungs. Findings may include a cough, sputum, dyspnea, chest
pain, cyanosis, rales, or edema.

c. Urinary tract. Findings may include urinary urgency, frequency, dysuria,
tenesmus, flank pain, oliguria, or anuria.

d. Gastrointestinal tract. Findings may include abdominal pain, nausea,
vomiting, diarrhea, constipation, or jaundice.

e. Central nervous system (CNS). Findings may include an altered sensorium,
headache, stiff neck, focal neurologic signs, photophobia, retinal hemor-
rhages, cotton wool spots, conjunctival petechiae, endophthalmitis, or
panophthalmitis.

C. Differential diagnoses include the following:
1. Viral diseases (e.g., influenza, dengue fever, coxsackie B virus infection)
2. Spirochetal diseases (e.g., syphilitic Jarisch-Herxheimer reaction, leptospirosis,
relapsing fever caused by Borrelia infection)
3. Rickettsial diseases (e.g., Rocky Mountain spotted fever, endemic typhus)
4. Protozoal diseases (e.g., Toxoplasma gondii infection, Trypanosoma cruzi
infection, Pneumocystis carinii infection, Plasmodium falciparum infection)
5. Endocrine diseases (e.g., adrenal insufficiency, thyroid storm)
6. Nonseptic causes of shock (e.g., cardiogenic shock, hypovolemic shock,
neurogenic shock, pulmonary embolism, cardiac tamponade, anaphylaxis,
dissecting aortic aneurysm)
Quick 	HIT
In an immunosuppressed
or neutropenic patient, a
localized area of painful
erythema may well indicate
an initial focal source, even
in the absence of localized
warmth and swelling and
irrespective of the absolute
white blood cell count.

Quick 	HIT
When the initial focus of in-
fection is difficult to discern,
one must strongly consider
the probability of infection
originating in the gastroin-
testinal tract (especially in
patients on chemotherapy).

Infectious Disease Emergencies
7. Collagen vascular diseases

## 8. Vasculitides

9. Thrombocytic thrombocytopenic purpura/hemolytic-uremic syndrome
D. Evaluation. There is no single reliable laboratory test for the diagnosis of sepsis.

Although the history and physical examination form the basis of the presumptive
diagnosis, the following additional testing is still considered essential.

1. Laboratory studies
a. Blood cultures and site-specific cultures (e.g., sputum, urine, cerebrospinal
fluid [CSF]) should be obtained prior to initiating empiric antibiotic therapy.

b. Gram staining of site-specific samples is indicated.

c. Serologic studies (e.g., counterimmune electrophoresis, latex agglutination)
are useful when infection with pneumococcus, Haemophilus influenzae ,
meningococcus, or group B streptococcus is suspected.

d. Complete blood count (CBC). Findings compatible with a diagnosis of sepsis
include leukocytosis, eosinopenia, anemia, leukopenia, and thrombocytopenia.

e. Urinalysis. Proteinuria is a finding that supports a diagnosis of sepsis.

f. Urine Legionella antigen. Positive result suggests current or past infection.

May be detectable in the urine as early as 3 days after onset of symptoms
g. Urine Streptococcus pneumoniae antigen is the most frequently encountered
agent of community-acquired pneumonia.

h. Coagulation profile. Findings that would support a diagnosis of sepsis
include a prolonged prothrombin time and thrombocytopenia.

i. Arterial blood gas profile. Findings in a patient with sepsis could include
hypoxemia and metabolic acidosis.

j. Serum biochemical profile. Supportive findings would include hypoferremia,
hyper- or hypoglycemia, hypocalcemia, hyperbilirubinemia, and azotemia.

k. Lactic acid. Mainly produced in the muscle and red blood cells under an-
aerobic conditions. Elevated levels (!1 mmol/L) can be a manifestation of
organ hypoperfusion in the presence or absence of hypotension and is an
important component of initial sepsis work-up. Note that elevated levels
occur not only in sepsis, but in heart, liver, and pulmonary disease.

l. Procalcitonin may be helpful to evaluate a seriously ill patient for systemic
bacterial infection. This test is best used during the first day of presentation
of illness and subsequently to monitor the response to treatment.

Systemic Inflammatory Response Syndrome Criteria:
1. Temperature !38C (101.4F) or "36C (96.8F)

## 2. Heart rate !90

3. Respiratory rate !20 or PaCo2 "32 mm Hg
4. While blood cells !12,000/L or "4,000/L, or !10% bandemia
PEARL	CLINICAL 	6-1
Factors Predisposing to Sepsis
a. Extremes of age
b. Iatrogenic procedures (e.g., indwelling catheters, surgical or invasive diagnostic procedures)
c. Medical conditions (e.g., cirrhosis, burns, multiple trauma, diabetes mellitus, cancer, complicated
pregnancy or delivery)
d. Social factors (e.g., alcoholism, substance abuse, intravenous drug abuse)
e. Immunosuppression (e.g., neutropenia, complement deficiencies, hypo- or agammaglobulinemia,
splenectomy, HIV infection)
f. Malnutrition
PEARL	CLINICAL 	6-2
154 	 	S T E P - U P T O E M E R G E N C Y M E D I C I N E

Infectious Disease Emergencies
2. Imaging studies. Site-specific radiographs, ultrasonograms, computed tomog-
raphy (CT) scans, or magnetic resonance imaging scans may be appropriate.

3. Other studies. Evaluation of site-specific samples obtained by biopsy or aspi-
ration may be helpful.

E. Therapy
1. Supportive measures
a. Mechanical ventilation is necessary for patients with respiratory insufficiency.

b. Volume replacement. Serial 500 mL boluses of normal saline are indicated
as initial therapy for sepsis or septic shock. Exercise caution and intubate
early with coexisting heart failure.

c. Pressor agents may also be required for patients who remain hypotensive
despite adequate fluid resuscitation or develop cardiogenic pulmonary edema.

i. Norepinephrine is the preferred vasopressor for the treatment of septic
shock. Acting both on both alpha-1 and beta-1 adrenergic receptors,
norepinephrine produces potent vasoconstriction as well as a modest
increase in cardiac output.

ii. Phenylephrine (Neo-Synephrine, Bayer, Leverkusen, Germany) has
purely alpha-adrenergic agonist activity and therefore results in vaso-
constriction with minimal cardiac inotropy or chronotropy. Useful for
patients with severe hypotension such as hyperdynamic sepsis, neu-
rologic disorders, or anesthesia-induced hypotension, or reserved for
patients in whom norepinephrine is contraindicated due to arrhythmias
or who have failed other therapies.

iii. Epinephrine is most often used for the treatment of anaphylaxis, as a
second-line agent after norepinephrine in septic shock.

iv. Dopamine is most often used as a second-line alternative to norepi-
nephrine in patients with absolute or relative bradycardia and a low
risk of tachyarrhythmias.

d. Transfusion
i. Pack red blood cell transfusion is indicated if the patient has hemoglo-
bin below 10 mg/dL or signs of hypoperfusion.

ii. Fresh frozen plasma transfusion is necessary for patients with depleted
levels of coagulation factors.

iii. Platelet transfusions may be indicated when the patient has
thrombocytopenia.

iv. Cryoprecipitate transfusions are indicated for patients with
hypofibrinogenemia.

e. Correction of metabolic abnormalities. Acidemia, hypoxia, hyper- or
hypoglycemia, electrolyte imbalances, and nutritional deficiencies must
be corrected.

f. Supportive pharmacologic therapy
i. Corticosteroids (hydrocortisone) may be indicated in the presence of
shock with poor response to fluids and vasopressors. Dexamethasone
should be considered before antibiotic administration for patients with
pneumococcal meningitis.

ii. Drotrecogin alpha (Xigris, Eli Lilly and Company, Indianapolis, IN):
Activated protein C is no longer used even in patients with severe
sepsis.

2. Specific measures
a. Source control. Focal causes of infection such as abscess or osteomyelitis
must be treated locally.

b. Antibiotic therapy. All patients with septic shock should receive empiric
antibiotic therapy as soon as possible. Once the source of the infection has
been identified, the spectrum of antibiotic coverage may be narrowed. Two
to three antibiotics should be given in cases of sepsis, with goal of adminis-
tration in less than 60 minutes from arrival.

i. Selecting empiric antibiotics. Knowledge of preexisting immune
dysfunction can be useful for targeting antibiotics for initial empiric
Quick 	HIT
Norepinephrine is the pre-
ferred vasopressor for the
treatment of septic shock.

I N F E C T I O U S D I S E A S E E M E R G E N C I E S 	 	155

Infectious Disease Emergencies
therapy (Table 6-1). Empiric therapy should be effective against gram-
positive and gram-negative bacteria, and given intravenously in the
maximum doses allowed.

ii. Specific drug combinations are summarized in Table 6-2.

F. 	Disposition. Clinically unstable patients must be treated in the intensive care unit
(ICU). Chemistries and CBC should be monitored closely in all patients. Serial
lactate or serial mixed venous oxygen saturation are used to follow response
to therapy.

G. Prevention
1. Gammaglobulin should be administered to hypo- or agammaglobulinemic
patients.

2. Pneumococcal vaccination and vaccination against H. influenzae are indicated
for geriatric patients and patients with certain chronic diseases. Further read-
ing is encouraged (Surviving Sepsis Campaign) at www.survivingsepsis.org.
```
